schools.summary: Israel encamped on the border of the promised land — 'pitched in the plains of Moab on this side Jordan by Jericho' (the Arboth Moab, once Moab's, taken from Moab by Sihon and from him by Israel, Israel now on the very border of Canaan parted only by the Jordan, Ellicott/Keil/Wesley); the forty years' wanderings ended, the people camped by Jericho till they should pass over to the conquest (Henry); the new and subtler danger — Satan, having assailed Israel in vain by force of arms (Sihon and Og), now attacking by another weapon, the curses and divinations of Balaam, the enemy beaten in open battle resorting to sorcery and false prophecy (Calvin); the danger of the curse met by the God who turns curses into blessings (Deut 23:5).

themes: on the border of the inheritance

schools.summary: Balak's observation of Israel's victories — 'Balak the son of Zippor saw all that Israel had done to the Amorites'; 'the Amorites' taking in both Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan, whose kingdoms Israel had lately overthrown (Num 21:24,35), Balak having seen or heard of these mighty victories (Gill); the force of the report — the two great Amorite kings, who had themselves taken much of Moab's land, destroyed by Israel, so what could Moab hope; Israel at the border, before it could be passed preparations on every side, among them the stirring of Moab's king (Keil); the conquests God gave Israel serving also to terrify their enemies, the report of his works going before his people; Balak's fear driving him to a vain and wicked remedy.

themes: saw all that Israel had done
the fall of Sihon and Og

schools.summary: Moab's groundless dread — 'Moab was sore afraid of the people, because they were many: and Moab was distressed' (the king, nobles, and people filled with terror at the multitude, fearing to be served as Sihon and Og, Gill); in truth no ground for the fear, for God had commanded 'Distress not the Moabites, neither contend with them in battle' (Deut 2:9), Israel having no commission to attack Moab (Ellicott); the dread foretold — generally that the fear of Israel should fall on all nations (Deut 2:25) and particularly of Moab (Ex 15:15, Wesley); the irony — Moab tormenting itself with a fear that need never have been, driven to seek the ruin of a people who would not have touched them, groundless fear breeding wicked counsel; the wicked pursued by terrors of their own imagining.

Moab's alliance with Midian — 'now shall this company lick up all that are round about us, as the ox licketh up the grass' (Moab picturing Israel as an ox devouring the grass, fearing the multitude would consume all the surrounding peoples); 'Moab said unto the elders of Midian' (Moab sending for and consulting the elders of Midian for the safety of both, Gill; these elders elsewhere called the kings and princes of Midian, Num 31:8, a divided people now united against the common enemy, Wesley); the close alliance of Moab and Midian running throughout, Balak himself perhaps of Midianite origin set over Moab (Ellicott); 'Balak... was king of the Moabites at that time,' the chief mover of the plot; peoples otherwise at odds leaguing together against the people of God.

themes: lick up all that are round about us

schools.summary: The sending for Balaam — 'he sent messengers unto Balaam the son of Beor to Pethor... there is a people come out from Egypt... they abide over against me' (Balak reporting Israel's coming and his peril, seeking Balaam's help); Balaam's mingled character — in some sort a prophet who knew the true God, called a prophet (2 Pet 2:16), yet one who had joined sorcery and divination to his gift and loved the wages of unrighteousness (2 Pet 2:15), a man between two worlds, knowing God yet serving gain (Clarke, Wesley); the ominous name from a root meaning 'to devour' or 'consume the people' (Ellicott); Balak's seeking of help, conscious of his weakness, springing from a principle good in itself but turned to evil, as Satan perverts good instincts (Calvin); Pethor by the river, far off, whence the famed diviner was fetched.

themes: unto Balaam the son of Beor
a people come out from Egypt

schools.summary: Balak's request and his faith in the curse — 'curse me this people; for they are too mighty for me... he whom thou blessest is blessed, and he whom thou cursest is cursed'; Balak owning he cannot overcome Israel by force and seeking to weaken them by a curse that he might smite and drive them out, the partnership 'thou by thy imprecations, and I by my sword' (Wesley); Balak undoubtedly believing in the real efficacy of Balaam's incantations, the power to bless and curse celebrated as a great and dreaded gift (Ellicott); Balaam one of the Moshelim, adding sorcery to the poetic gift (Clarke); the heathen darkness — thinking the favour or curse of God could be bought by a diviner's art, as if blessing lay in the prophet's mouth rather than God's hand; no curse falling on those whom God has blessed, the design vain from the first (Num 23:8).

themes: they are too mighty for me

schools.summary: The messengers bearing the diviner's fee — 'the elders of Moab and the elders of Midian departed with the rewards of divination in their hand'; the envoys princes and nobles, men of rank sent on so weighty an errand, the importance Balak set on the business appearing in their quality (Gill); 'the rewards of divination' — whoever consulted a prophet took a present, on which such men lived, but here more than a mere gift, perhaps all needful to procure and pay for the divination, the hire of the curse (Clarke, Ellicott); the close league of Moab and Midian running through the story; the moral — Balaam a man to be hired, his prophecy a thing for sale, the wages of unrighteousness which he loved (2 Pet 2:15) the bait that drew him, the fee in their hand the snare to his soul; the love of money the root of his undoing.

themes: the elders of Moab and Midian

schools.summary: Balaam's consulting the LORD, his true character disclosed — 'I will bring you word again, as the LORD shall speak unto me'; the name Jehovah, the true God, whom Balaam knew and had been wont to consult, receiving oracles (Clarke, Wesley), so likely no mere sorcerer but a man to whom God really spoke (Calvin); the night the season of God's dreams (Wesley); yet his very willingness to inquire betraying his character — as a prophet of the LORD he must have known that to curse God's chosen people was a plain sin, and ought to have refused outright without asking, his keeping the envoys and seeking leave showing his heart already inclined to go if he might (Ellicott); at once disposed to do their business, the consulting not the reluctance of conscience but the angling of covetousness for a permission (Gill); to parley with a known sin and ask leave for it already half to have yielded.

themes: as the LORD shall speak unto me

schools.summary: God's searching question — 'God came unto Balaam, and said, What men are these with thee?'; God coming in a dream of the night, as to Abimelech and Laban (Gen 20:3, 31:24), condescending to speak even to such a man (Gill); the question compared to God's searching questions to Elijah, 'What doest thou here?' (1 Kgs 19:9), and to Hezekiah, 'What said these men, and whence came they?' (Isa 39:3), not that God needed information but that the question might probe the conscience (Ellicott); God asking not for his own knowledge but that Balaam, repeating the matter aloud in God's presence, might see and be ashamed of the sin and folly of offering his service, the question meant to convict and lay a foundation for the answer (Wesley); the all-knowing God asking not to learn but to teach and to try.

themes: What men are these with thee?

schools.summary: Balaam's answer to God — 'Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, hath sent unto me' (Balaam answering the question God had put, naming the king who had sent and the errand, Gill); Balaam, knowing he could not deceive the omniscient God, telling the matter plainly, speaking truthfully of the business whatever the corruption of his desire; in being made to rehearse the errand aloud in God's presence, given to hear from his own mouth the wickedness of what he was asked — to curse a whole people for a king's fear and a diviner's fee — the bare telling of it ought to have shamed him; the man stating his case fairly yet without any sign of the reluctance conscience should have felt, laying the temptation before God almost as one seeking leave; God dealing with Balaam by drawing the matter out of his own lips.

themes: Balak the king of Moab hath sent

schools.summary: Balaam rehearsing Balak's request — 'come now, curse me them; peradventure I shall be able to overcome them, and drive them out'; Balaam relating Balak's words faithfully and exactly, knowing he could not deceive the omniscient God, hiding nothing (Gill); the description of Israel 'a people come out of Egypt... it covereth the face of the earth' (Ellicott's rendering), the multitude that filled Balak with dread named again; the curse sought as a means to overcome and drive out a people Balak could not face in arms, the diviner's word to do what the king's sword could not; in rehearsing the matter Balaam laying the whole temptation open before God, so having no excuse of ignorance when God should forbid; the folly of thinking to overthrow by a hired curse the people whom God brought out of Egypt.

themes: a people come out of Egypt
overcome them and drive them out

schools.summary: God's flat prohibition — 'Thou shalt not go with them; thou shalt not curse the people: for they are blessed'; God forbidding both the going on this errand and the cursing, the precise sense 'thou shalt not go with them to curse,' for Balaam might lawfully go (as he afterward did by God's bidding) but never to curse (Clarke); the all-sufficient ground 'for they are blessed' — God peremptorily removing all doubt because it was unlawful and impossible to curse those whom God had blessed, the blessing lying on Israel, no diviner's word reversing it (Calvin); the heart of the story — the people of God standing under his blessing, that blessing irreversible, what God has blessed no man or power of darkness can curse (Rom 8:31); a true prophet doing no more than witnessing God's grace, unable to bestow or withdraw it at will (Calvin).

themes: thou shalt not go with them
thou shalt not curse the people

schools.summary: Balaam's partial report — 'Get you into your land: for the LORD refuseth to give me leave to go with you'; Balaam sending the messengers home, owning the LORD would not permit him to go; the telling defect — Balaam told the princes less than God had told him, God having said 'thou shalt not curse the people, for they are blessed' (v12), but Balaam reporting only that the LORD 'refuseth to give me leave to go,' saying nothing of the blessing of Israel nor the unlawfulness of cursing (Ainsworth, noted by the witnesses); by lessening the word of God, leaving Balak room to hope the prohibition was only of the going, not absolute, half-opening the door he should have shut; the working of covetousness — a man truly resolved would have told the whole truth, but Balaam, loving the reward, framing his answer so as not to cut off hope of a second offer.

themes: the LORD refuseth to give me leave
he told them less than God told

schools.summary: The report carried back to Balak — 'Balaam refuseth to come with us'; the princes reporting only Balaam's refusal, as of a man who would not come, not the LORD's prohibition nor the blessing of Israel; Satan's practice against the word of God, to lessen it from hand to hand till he bring it to naught — God told Balaam the full truth, Balaam told the princes less, the princes tell Balak less still, at each remove the word weakened, the blessing and the unlawfulness dropping out (Clarke, after Ainsworth); because Balaam had not told the real ground 'for they are blessed,' Balak left to suppose the refusal a matter of reluctance or price, to be overcome by stronger inducements, the door left open for a richer embassy (Ellicott); the princes not staying to argue, perceiving him determined (Gill); the plain word of God diminished as it passes through unfaithful mouths.

themes: Balaam refuseth to come with us
the word lessened from hand to hand
a softened report breeds fresh attempts

schools.summary: The second, more honourable embassy — 'Balak sent yet again princes, more, and more honourable than they'; the new envoys more in number and greater in rank, princes of the first quality in Balak's court, sent on the supposition that Balaam had felt himself not honoured enough and might be won by a more dignified suit (Gill); it were well if we were as earnest and constant in a good work, notwithstanding discouragements, as Balak in pursuing this evil one, the diligence of the wicked rebuking the slackness of the godly (Henry); the ungodly, however humbly they implore, not laying aside their pride but trusting their grandeur and honours can prevail, Balak thinking to bend even the prophet by the weight of his princes and greatness of his offers (Calvin); the second embassy a fresh and sharper trial to the covetous Balaam, the bait richer and the honour greater.

The second message's entreaty — 'Thus saith Balak... Let nothing, I pray thee, hinder thee from coming unto me'; the king pressing Balaam to let no obstacle stand in the way, to overcome whatever scruple had held him back, the words full of urgency and entreaty; though men of rank and high office, the princes not declining so mean an errand nor thinking it below them to wait upon a soothsayer, the importance Balak set on the business and the honour in which divination was held making even great men its suitors (Gill); the flattery and pressure — Balak treating Balaam as a man of the highest consequence whose reluctance must be coaxed away, the king stooping to entreat the diviner; the danger — a man already inclined now plied with flattering importunity, his vanity courted and his covetousness inflamed, the second suit a heavier trial than the first.

themes: let nothing hinder thee from coming

schools.summary: The bait of honour and reward — 'I will promote thee unto very great honour, and I will do whatsoever thou sayest unto me: come therefore... curse me this people'; Balak offering to advance Balaam to high dignity in his court, perhaps to make him a great officer or prime minister, dangling rank and station (Gill); 'I will do whatsoever thou sayest' — a blank warrant, undertaking to grant whatever he should ask, the bait as large as the diviner's greed could wish; the two-edged temptation — as Balak first laid a snare for Balaam's covetousness with the rewards of divination (v7), now doubling it with honour as well as wealth, pressing both the love of money and of preferment; all the honour hung on the one forbidden thing, the cursing of God's blessed people; a fierce assault on the weak point of a man who loved the wages of unrighteousness.

themes: I will promote thee to great honour

schools.summary: Balaam's fine profession — 'if Balak would give me his house full of silver and gold, I cannot go beyond the word of the LORD my God, to do less or more'; the saying true in itself and admirable on its face; the truth in it — Balaam knew God too well to think he could reverse his purposes, and respected him too much to act without leave, though covetous yet having a real fear of God (Clarke); the suspicion of his sincerity — the words perhaps no more than an ostentatious show of disinterestedness and superiority to gain, the very protest of one who longed for the silver and gold, or springing from his conscious inability to curse whom God had blessed, dressed up as virtue (Ellicott); the tell-tale 'the word of the LORD my God,' naming God as his yet hoping that word might change; fair professions of submission cloaking a heart still set on forbidden gain.

themes: his house full of silver and gold
I cannot go beyond the word of the LORD

schools.summary: Balaam inquiring again — 'tarry ye also here this night, that I may know what the LORD will say unto me more'; Balaam keeping this embassy as the first and proposing to consult God again, as if there were more to learn; the charitable construction that he did not know but God might make some further discovery, and so might innocently seek more light (Clarke); but the sharper judgment — this showing his strong inclination to go, when he might and ought to have given a flat denial, God's mind already fully known (Gill), and the vanity of it, Balaam knowing God is not a man that he should lie or repent (Num 23:19) yet indulging the empty hope that God might change his word and let him curse (Ellicott); a heart truly submitted would have rested in the first prohibition, but Balaam, hankering after the reward, going back as if to wring a different answer; to ask God again for leave to do what he has forbidden not piety but the importunity of a divided heart.

themes: tarry ye also here this night
what the LORD will say unto me more
the importunity of a divided heart

schools.summary: God's permission given in judgment — 'if the men come to call thee, rise up, and go with them; but yet the word which I shall say unto thee, that shalt thou do'; God allowing the journey on condition the men summon him, yet this leave no token of God's pleasure; the binding limit — Balaam might go but only to speak what God should put in his mouth, the permission chaining him to God's purpose so that his going could not curse but must bless; God, far from deceitful, justly letting hypocrites be deluded by their own quibbles, Balaam wishing God to belie himself, God giving a leave that would only ensnare him (Calvin); the going marked with divine displeasure, for Balaam wished for honours and rewards to fulfil the king's desire, the permission a giving-up of the man to his chosen way (Clarke); when a man presses for leave to sin, God may grant his desire to his own hurt (Ps 81:12).

debates.question: How is God's permission to Balaam to go (v20) consistent with his anger that Balaam went (v22) and his prior prohibition (v12)?

sides.position: the permission was a judicial giving-up, not an approval — God let the covetous prophet have the leave he craved, to his own ensnaring and hurt, while chaining him to speak only God's word; the anger fell because Balaam went in the wrong spirit, eager for reward, and (v21-22) without even the condition God set

themes: if the men come to call thee, rise up and go
the word I shall say, that shalt thou do
given up to his heart's lust

schools.summary: Balaam's eager setting-out — 'Balaam rose up in the morning, and saddled his ass, and went with the princes of Moab'; rising up early and not even waiting for the princes to call him as God's permission required (v20), his rising betimes and saddling his own beast showing how eager he was to be gone and how intent upon the journey (Gill); God had made the journey hang on the men's summons, but Balaam, instead of waiting to be called, went forward of his own accord, outrunning the condition and the leave, his haste the haste of greed hurrying to the reward; a man truly reluctant would have waited and gone slowly, but Balaam sprang up like one glad of his errand; the manner of an action disclosing the heart, his eagerness showing his soul set on the gain, going after the wages of unrighteousness (2 Pet 2:15); his very promptness his sin.

themes: Balaam rose up in the morning

schools.summary: God's anger and the angel-adversary — 'God's anger was kindled because he went: and the angel of the LORD stood in the way for an adversary against him'; though God had permitted the journey (v20), his anger now burning because Balaam went of his own accord, not waiting to be called as the permission required, and in the wrong spirit, hankering after the reward, the way the apostle calls the way of unrighteousness (2 Pet 2:15, Wesley); how God could be angry at what he allowed — God permits much he does not approve, the leave a judicial giving-up, the anger just, falling on the corrupt heart (Calvin); the Hebrew 'because he was going,' the continuous act, Balaam deliberately proceeding in defiance of the warnings (Ellicott); the angel who elsewhere guarded Israel now standing against the hireling as a satan, a withstander, sword in hand; the diviner who thought to control unseen powers confronted by the Power he could not see.

themes: God's anger was kindled because he went
the angel of the LORD for an adversary

schools.summary: The ass seeing what the prophet could not — 'the ass saw the angel of the LORD standing in the way, and his sword drawn in his hand... and the ass turned aside... and Balaam smote the ass'; the dumb beast perceiving the heavenly adversary with drawn sword while Balaam, the prophet who boasted of visions, saw nothing, his eyes held by the providence of God that he might be rebuked and shamed by his own ass (Gill); when God granted such visions, those alone particularly concerned saw them, others seeing nothing, as the men with Daniel and Saul (Dan 10:7, Acts 9:7, Clarke); the ass's right instinct, turning aside into the field to flee the drawn sword, obeying the warning her master would not heed; Balaam's blind cruelty, beating the creature for the very act that saved his life; God making a dumb ass wiser than the seer, covetousness darkening the man below his own beast.

themes: his sword drawn in his hand
the beast sees, the seer is blind

schools.summary: The angel in the narrow vineyard path — 'the angel of the LORD stood in a path of the vineyards, a wall being on this side, and a wall on that side' (better the hollow or sunken pass of the vineyards, a track between two walls or fences, Ellicott); the ass, before Balaam could turn her back from the field, having got into this narrow path between the vineyards (Gill); the tightening of the angel's opposition — in the open field the ass could turn aside freely, but here hemmed by walls little room to escape the drawn sword, the angel pressing his adversary into a narrower place; God's dealing with sinners (after Ainsworth) — first mildly showing his rod and letting them pass, then as they persist straitening their way and pressing harder; the further Balaam pressed against God's will, the narrower and more dangerous his road, obstinacy meeting ever closer hindrance (cf. Hos 2:6).

themes: a wall on this side and that

schools.summary: The ass crushing Balaam's foot — 'when the ass saw the angel of the LORD, she thrust herself unto the wall, and crushed Balaam's foot against the wall: and he smote her again'; seeing the angel again with drawn sword, frightened, hemmed in the narrow path with no way to turn, she pressed herself hard against the wall to avoid him (Gill); in squeezing past the angel along the wall, she jammed her master's foot against the stone, the man now feeling in his own flesh the consequence of his obstinate course; Balaam's renewed cruelty, still blind to the cause and angered by the pain, beating the poor beast a second time for fleeing a danger he could not see; the deepening warning — first the ass turned into the field, now he suffers bodily hurt, God's hindrances growing heavier as the prophet persists; the crushed foot a mercy in disguise, a lesser pain to save from a greater ruin.

themes: she thrust herself unto the wall

schools.summary: The way wholly barred — 'the angel of the LORD went further, and stood in a narrow place, where was no way to turn either to the right hand or to the left'; a narrower place than the vineyard path, the angel pressing closer still, the ass unable to turn aside in any direction, the way wholly blocked (Gill); the angel's successive stands a picture of God's dealings with persistent sinners — first mildly shaking his rod and letting them go untouched, then standing in a straiter place and pressing harder, at last barring the way altogether, that they be brought to a stand and forced to consider (Clarke, after Ainsworth); the road closing step by step — the open field, the walled path, now the strait place with no escape, Balaam's obstinacy bringing him to a dead stop; the mercy in the severity, God shutting up the way not to destroy at once but to halt the sinner and turn him back, the closed road a barrier of grace.

themes: no way to turn right or left

schools.summary: The ass falling and Balaam's rage — 'when the ass saw the angel of the LORD, she fell down under Balaam: and Balaam's anger was kindled, and he smote the ass with a staff'; the third time the ass saw the angel (who seems to have appeared and disappeared, seen by the beast at each new stand though not by the man), now with no way to turn she sank down under her rider, unable to go on (Gill); for the third time Balaam struck the creature, now with a staff, in a heat of rage, his own anger kindled even as God's anger was kindled against him (v22) — but his against a faithful beast, God's against a faithless prophet; the seer, blind to the angel, raging against the only one in that path who saw clearly, his fury on the innocent creature that thrice saved his life; thrice warned, he never once stopped to ask the cause but only beat the harder, obstinacy making him cruel and blind.

themes: he smote the ass with a staff
raging against the one that saw

schools.summary: The speaking of the ass — 'the LORD opened the mouth of the ass, and she said unto Balaam, What have I done unto thee, that thou hast smitten me these three times?'; God conferring on her the power of speech and reasoning for that time (Wesley), a supernatural work that a dumb creature lacking the organs of speech should utter articulate words (Gill); the wonder not in the beast but in the power of God — if the ass had opened her own mouth we might marvel, but when God opens the mouth an ass can speak as plainly as a man (Clarke); the meekness of her words, pleading her cause gently, the beast reasoning more justly than the man; God making a brute creature reprove the seer for his blind cruelty, the proud prophet brought low by his own ass.

Calvin defends the plain literal miracle against scoffers who ridicule the passage as a fable; the New Testament settles it — 'the dumb ass speaking with man's voice forbad the madness of the prophet' (2 Pet 2:16).

debates.question: Was the speaking of the ass a literal miracle or a subjective/visionary experience?

sides.position: a literal miracle wrought by God, who opened the beast's mouth — the wonder lying in God's power, not the ass; confirmed by 2 Pet 2:16, 'the dumb ass speaking with man's voice'
some critics, while admitting the miraculous character of the events, make parts of the incident subjective or visionary rather than literal

themes: the LORD opened the mouth of the ass
What have I done unto thee?
the dumb beast rebukes the prophet

schools.summary: Balaam's furious answer — 'Because thou hast mocked me: I would there were a sword in mine hand, for now would I kill thee'; 'mocked' rather 'defiled' or 'abused' me, by running him against the wall and casting him in the dust, Balaam blaming the beast for the hurts his own obstinacy caused (Gill); the violence of his wish to kill his own faithful ass, the sharp irony that a sword was indeed near — drawn in the angel's hand against Balaam himself, though he could not see it; the strange thing — Balaam not much terrified or astonished at his ass speaking, perhaps accustomed in his dealings with evil spirits to such uncanny voices, or his passion so blinding him that his rage swallowed his reason (Wesley); the depth of his folly, quarrelling with a beast and threatening to kill the creature that saved his life while blind to the angel; a heart set on its own way past astonishment and shame.

themes: would there were a sword in mine hand

schools.summary: The ass's reasoning plea — 'Am not I thine ass, upon which thou hast ridden ever since I was thine unto this day? was I ever wont to do so unto thee? And he said, Nay'; she appeals to her long and faithful service, 'ever since I was thine' meaning 'all thy life long,' the Targums 'from thy youth unto this day' (Ellicott), reasoning that having carried him faithfully all these years and never behaved so before, her strange conduct now must have some cause he had not considered; the wonder that the ass not only spoke but reasoned, replying as if she understood his words and could argue her case (Gill); the justice of her reasoning — a faithful servant's sudden change should make a wise master ask the reason, not reach for a sword, the beast arguing more soundly than the seer; Balaam forced to own 'Nay,' his own mouth granting her plea and convicting his rashness; the prophet the scholar and the brute the teacher.

themes: was I ever wont to do so
the beast reasons, the seer confesses

schools.summary: Balaam's eyes opened — 'the LORD opened the eyes of Balaam, and he saw the angel of the LORD standing in the way, and his sword drawn in his hand: and he bowed down his head, and fell flat on his face'; his eyes, which had been held (not struck blind, for he saw his ass and the way, but restrained from seeing the angel), now opened (Gill); the keenness of our senses not only given by God but sustained or extinguished by his secret power, Balaam seeing nothing till God granted a new act of perception (Calvin); the very sight that terrified the ass now bursting upon the prophet, the drawn sword that had been all along against him revealed; 'he bowed down his head, and fell flat on his face' in token of reverence and submission (Wesley), the proud seer who raged at his ass falling prostrate; Balaam more blind than his beast, outdone by a dumb ass until God opened his eyes.

themes: the LORD opened the eyes of Balaam
the angel with his sword drawn
the seer beholds what his ass saw

schools.summary: The angel's rebuke — 'Wherefore hast thou smitten thine ass these three times? behold, I went out to withstand thee, because thy way is perverse before me'; the angel taking up the very words and grievance the ass had spoken (v28), charging Balaam with the wrong done his faithful beast, the man called to account for his blind cruelty; the deeper charge — the angel declaring himself the adversary in the path and naming the cause, Balaam's way perverse, crooked, contrary to God; the phrase perhaps meaning 'the way leads to destruction in my sight,' a road headlong to ruin (Ellicott), the perverseness springing from covetousness, the love of the reward bending his way crooked (Wesley); not the ass but Balaam who deserved the blows, his perverse course bringing all the trouble; the mercy in the reproof, the angel explaining the cause that Balaam may understand and turn.

themes: wherefore hast thou smitten thine ass
I went out to withstand thee
thy way is perverse before me

schools.summary: The ass's turning that saved Balaam's life — 'the ass saw me, and turned from me these three times: unless she had turned from me, surely now also I had slain thee, and saved her alive'; the beast having seen the angel and turned away not from waywardness but to avoid the drawn sword, her seeming disobedience her wisdom; had the ass gone on as Balaam wished, the angel would have killed the man and spared the beast, the very turning for which Balaam beat her standing between him and death; Balaam's anger against the ass unjust, her turning wholly for his benefit not her own, he punishing his preserver (Wesley); how often the meanest animals and trivial occurrences are made instruments of preserving our lives and saving our souls (Clarke); the seer owing his life to the beast he despised, God's providence using a dumb ass to shield him; men often beating the very mercies that save them.

themes: the ass saw me, and turned
unless she had turned, I had slain thee

schools.summary: Balaam's shallow confession — 'I have sinned; for I knew not that thou stoodest in the way against me: now therefore, if it displease thee, I will get me back again'; the words sounding well but shallow, Balaam owning his sin chiefly in the beating of the ass and going on against the unseen angel, not truly sensible of the deeper sin, the covetousness of his heart and his wicked intention in undertaking the errand at all (Gill); the excuse 'I knew not that thou stoodest in the way,' pleading ignorance as though his fault were only not seeing the angel, the confession hedged with self-excuse; the conditional offer 'if it displease thee' — a proof he still feared God and would abandon the journey if God were displeased (Clarke), yet hollow, for a truly changed heart would have turned back gladly, not merely offered if pressed; a confession of the lips that still clings to the gain, like Saul's 'I have sinned' without true repentance.

themes: I knew not that thou stoodest in the way
if it displease thee, I will get me back
confession of the lips, not the heart

schools.summary: The renewed permission and restraint — 'Go with the men: but only the word that I shall speak unto thee, that thou shalt speak'; not a command but a permission, a leaving of Balaam to his own heart's lust, God again as it were ironically allowing him to do what could not be done without sin, giving the covetous man his way to his own hurt (Gill, Calvin); the command of v20 repeated, the unrighteous prophet punished by being made to reap the fruit of his own perversity, going since he would go but to no profit (Ellicott); the binding restraint — Balaam chained to God's word, his mouth not his own, to utter only what God puts in it and so to bless where he was hired to curse; God turning the very going of Balaam to the honour of Israel, making the hireling His mouthpiece, the curse robbed of all power before it is sought; God may grant a sinful desire yet so order it that the man gains nothing but shame.

themes: only the word that I shall speak

schools.summary: Balak going out to meet Balaam — 'when Balak heard that Balaam was come, he went out to meet him unto a city of Moab... in the utmost coast'; better 'the city of Moab,' on the Arnon at the very border (Sihon having taken Moab's land down to that river), not far from the camp of Israel whom Balak desired cursed (Ellicott, Wesley); Balak's eagerness, the king himself going out to meet Balaam, a mark of honour and impatience, coming to the frontier to greet his hoped-for deliverer; the two confederate enemies brought together, differing in expectations — Balak confident of success, Balaam already humbled and doubtful (Henry); the meeting representing the spirit of all devoted to superstition without true fear of God, cringing to their false prophets and trusting the diviner rather than the living God (Calvin); the irony — Balak running to embrace a man bound by God to bless, hastening to his own disappointment.

themes: Balak heard that Balaam was come
a city of Moab in the utmost coast
king and diviner meet against Israel

schools.summary: Balak's reproach to Balaam — 'Did I not earnestly send unto thee to call thee? wherefore camest thou not unto me? am I not able indeed to promote thee to honour?'; Balak upon meeting complaining that Balaam had not come at the first summons but needed a second embassy, reproaching the prophet's delay as mere reluctance or holding-out for more (Gill); the renewed bait — pressing again the promise of advancement, asserting his power and willingness to enrich and exalt Balaam, dangling once more the lure that had drawn the covetous prophet; Balak's misjudgment — supposing Balaam's slowness a bargaining for greater reward, never dreaming the true cause was God's prohibition, reading the hesitation by the measure of his own greed; Balak boasting of his power to honour a man whom God alone could prosper or abase, who could speak no word but what God gave.

themes: did I not earnestly send to call thee?
am I not able to promote thee?
the bait of honour pressed again

schools.summary: Balaam's sobered profession — 'have I now any power at all to say any thing? the word that God putteth in my mouth, that shall I speak'; Balaam owning plainly he has no power of his own to bless or curse but must speak only what God gives, his mouth not his own whatever Balak may hope; the noble resolution to which he was faithful — though he longed to please the king and gain the wealth and honour, he would not and could not displease God to win them (Clarke); yet the profession, true as it is, from a man whose heart still hankered after the reward, the bondage to God's word forced on him not freely embraced; the comfort for Israel — the hired diviner brought to curse confessing before the king that he can say nothing but what God puts in his mouth, and God would put only blessing there; the enemy's own prophet disclaiming all power against God's people.

themes: lo, I am come unto thee
have I any power to say anything?
the word God putteth in my mouth
bound to speak only God's word

schools.summary: The journey to Kirjath-huzoth — 'Balaam went with Balak, and they came unto Kirjathhuzoth'; the name meaning a 'city of streets,' whether a proper name or a description, a town to which Balak brought the prophet on the way to his purpose (Gill, Calvin); Balaam honourably and sumptuously received, the king sparing no cost to welcome and gratify the diviner on whom he set his hopes, the feasting and gifts the king's courting of the prophet (Calvin); the danger — the covetous man, already humbled by the angel and bound to God's word, now plied with the flattery of a royal entertainment, his weak point assailed by honour and good cheer; God letting Balaam go this far and be feasted only that the blessing of Israel might be the more conspicuous when it came, pronounced by a prophet the king had honoured and hired; the pomp unable to alter the word God would give.

Balak's sacrifices and feast — 'Balak offered oxen and sheep, and sent to Balaam, and to the princes that were with him'; the word perhaps meaning simply 'slew' (Ellicott), the witnesses dividing on the purpose — either for sacrifice or for a feast (Gill), or as sacrifice offered to gain the favour of Balak's gods and perhaps to propitiate Jehovah too, that the cursing of Israel might be accomplished (Clarke); of the slain beasts, flesh sent to Balaam and the envoys, a feast of welcome and honour, the king entertaining his hired prophet and his princes; the mingling of religion and policy — Balak joining sacrifice to feasting, courting his gods and his diviner together; the vanity — thinking to win by sacrifices the cursing of a people God had blessed, as if the favour of heaven could be bought to a wicked end; the pomp unable to change the coming word.

themes: sent to Balaam and the princes
worship and feasting attend the plot
the favour of heaven not bought to evil

schools.summary: The going up to Bamoth-Baal to view Israel — 'Balak took Balaam, and brought him up into the high places of Baal, that thence he might see the utmost part of the people'; Bamoth-Baal, a height consecrated to Baal (Baal-Peor, the god of Moab), the first eminence whence the Israelite camp could be seen (Wesley, Ellicott); 'that thence he might see the utmost part of the people' — Balak bringing Balaam where he could view the camp, at least its outermost part, on the heathen supposition that the diviner must have his object in sight to curse it, lest the curse miss those out of view (Clarke, Wesley, Hengstenberg); the darkness of the notion — that a curse could be aimed by the eye and cast only on what was seen; the unintended fitness — from this high place set to curse, Balaam would be made to bless, looking down on the tents of Jacob and pronouncing them goodly (Num 24:5); the place of intended cursing becoming the place of God's blessing.

themes: see the utmost part of the people
the place of cursing becomes the place of blessing
